speaker: interviewer
text: The date is September 25th, 1996. The survivor is Bernhard Storch. Last name at birth, Sztorch. Interviewer, Martha Frazer. The city and state are South Nyack, New York. Country, United States of America. The language is English. My name is Martha Frazer-- M-A-R-T-H-A F-R-A-Z-E-R. Today is September 25th, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Bernhard Storch in South Nyack, New York, the United States of America, in the English language. Please state and spell your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bernhard Storch. B-E-R-N-H-A-R-D. Sztorch-- S-Z-T-O-R-C-H. That's the way it was spelled in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell it in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the United States, it's Bernhard the way I spelled it previously. And it's Storch-- S-T-O-R-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your birth date and current age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Birth date is November-- November 19th-- November 10th, 1922. And age will be, what, 74, right? [LAUGHS] Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, on your citizenship papers, your birth date is listed as 1920.

speaker: interviewee
text: On my citizen papers, yes, the age is listed 1920. And the reason for it was that I wanted to get out from the army. Otherwise, I would have to be-- able to-- I would have to stay another two years. So we changed it from '22 to '20. That was the only reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was done in 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was done in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you please state and spell your city and country of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: The city pronounced is Bochnia-- B-O-N-I-A. And it's about 33 kilometers south of Cracow. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Country? Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: I've also seen it spelled B-O-C-H-N-I-A. have you seen it spelled that way also?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-H?

speaker: interviewer
text: N-I-A.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the way I spelled it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I'm not sure if you did.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's, that's the way I spelled it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Could you please describe the town for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the town was a small town-- actually a population of 2,000. And-- I mean, not 2,000, 20,000. I'm sorry, 20,000. And it's a very nice old city. And there, there were 2,500 Jews living there, and the rest were Christian. And the main religion was Catholic. There were some Protestants there too, but a very, very small minority of them. And the, the town itself was a beautiful town and a very historical town. And it's a recreational town, actually, because we had a big river there. And a lot of people from, from city, from the metropolitan areas, came and vacationed there. And it was-- it was in a valley, sort of, but it was surrounded with hills. They were hilly sites. And so I remember it was a beautiful city, beautiful city. Last time I did see it, it was 1945. That was the last time I was in Poland, was in-- in my city, was 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were most of the people in the town farmers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- the-- the in the town itself, there was all kind of little industries and businesses, but the surrounding area was mostly farmers and forests. But mostly farmers. There were fruit farmers and all other farming-- vegetables, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please describe your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family consisted of my father, my mother, and I had five brothers originally. One of them, unfortunately, passed away. And that was 1932, as far as I remember. 1932, yes-- that was the oldest one. And my father was a businessman. My mother, of course, was a housewife. And we-- as far as the household itself, it was a religious-- a religious home. And there was no conservatism in Europe, but it was orthodox-- strict orthodox and just plain orthodox. And that's where I came from, from a orthodox family. Which could be easily imagined the way we have here conservatism. And as far as the people living in town, it was a very nice community, actually. And I myself, as far as I remember-- don't forget, we're talking here way back. I myself did not feel any antisemitism in city itself. I was going to a Catholic school. And because schools were public and were run by the government, the majority were Catholic. So it was a Catholic school, but it was not conducted by priests or such. Religion was taught in school, and each denomination-- domination had their own preachers. In other words, the Catholic had a priest, and it was conducted every, every week. And it was conducted two hours a day-- a week. And the Jewish people had-- they did not have a rabbi, but we had a religious-- a religion-- professor of religion. And by the way, I still remember his name. His name was Dr. Landfish. He--

speaker: interviewer
text: And he came into the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came into the school at the same time-- at the same time when the priest came in. I don't remember exact date-- was it Tuesday, or Thursday, or whatever. But he came in at the same time the, the priest came in. He came in. And also, we had a Protestant children there, so they had their own preacher there. And when religion class started, the Jewish kids left the class, because we were the minority. In my class, we had nine Jewish kids. And the evangelists, which left-- the Protestant-- but that was evangelic religion, as far as I remember-- left that class. And they had their own classrooms. And everybody was taught for those two hours in different classes. But everybody was taught religion, and that was just the basic religion. That was basic religion. Hebrew education was taught in cheder, the way they set. But it was a Hebrew school, compared to the United States. It was a Hebrew school. And we had a preacher-- not a preacher, but a teacher there-- and-- orthodox teacher, of course-- and we were taught everything from A to B. And as far as myself, I was going to Hebrew school. And went all the way down until [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after my bar mitzvah, even. And the-- it was very, very strict. It was a lot of hours, as a matter of fact. It was not only Sunday. After school, we had to go to the Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the religion was taught there, and everything. And I went all the way down to [HEBREW]. All right? Not Russia, but [HEBREW]. [HEBREW]. So I knew Hebrew. I knew how to read the Torah. Let's put it this way. I knew how to read the Torah. So that was-- you know, that was education. The education itself was very thorough education. I went to public school, of course. And as far as graduation, I remember I graduated in 1937.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was nice. Let's put it this way. It was nice. We had sports and everything else, and I participated in sport. I was very good in sports. I participated in every sport there and was placed on the first team-- my school team. Mostly soccer, of course. There was no-- and volleyball. That was a big thing. And hockey during the winter. And as I say, I-- it was-- it was-- it was good. It was good. I did not feel any antisemitism myself. Maybe if you would have asked my father or, or my grandfather, they would probably have different opinions. I, as a child, did not felt that. So that's-- I have good memories. Let's put it this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't recall any fighting among the children over religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not-- no. There was no fighting over religion at all in my school, in my class. I had no, no such thing like fighting over religion. I never was called, you know-- yeah, yeah. They would mention, you know, like [POLISH], which meant Jew. You know, something was said, but not in any derogatory manner. Because we marched together in ev-- whenever there was a, you know, national holiday, or something like that, everybody dressed up the same way, and everyone marched. But, of course, we had in our class-- as I remember, we had in our class Hasidic children with payots. They were not bothered. They were not bothered in class. But don't forget, my town was a very modern town. If you would go to Poland in a smaller town, you would have a different opinion of it. But I'm talking strictly from my town. And it was a modern city, and we lived peacefully together. Our neighbors and everything was-- were fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of neighborhood did you live in? What were their houses like? Your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: My house was a single family home. Was a single family home, and it was Jews and Christian together. In other words, the first house was a Jewish house. The second house was a-- was a Christian house. The third house was a Jewish house, which was mine. The fourth was, again, a Christian house, and so on. And we had no animosities between ourselves. They knew when the holidays are. And as a matter of fact, we sometimes even invite the neighbors for the holidays and vice versa. Of course, we couldn't eat any, any meat in their house, because the house was strictly kosher. But they liked our food, and they were waiting for it. And it was fine. We played with the kids there, with the neighbors' children. And as a matter of fact, one of my neighbors, which after the war, I came to visit, to see-- not to visit her, but to see if there's any survivors from my-- from my family. And the first step which I did is to my neighbor. She was alive. Her husband was not. And I met two of the children. Two of them passed. She had four children. Two of them died during the war. And we had-- she knew, because they were very close neighbors with my parents, she knew that I was in Siberia at that time. But she didn't know that I was in the Polish army. And she didn't know if I survived or not. So she almost fainted when she saw me, seriously. So, you know. Of course, my house was demolished. I did not have a house. When I came in, there was no home there anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll talk about your visit back--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK

speaker: interviewer
text: --home when we--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --get to 1945. Could you tell us a little more about your father? His business? What, what did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a businessman. And what he had, he had two trucks-- two trucks. One was a moving truck, you know, for people to move from one city or from one place to the other. Like a moving truck. And the other one was a general transportation truck to move goods from the railroad station to the businesspeople. And he had that in 1932. In 1932, and 1933, '34. Later, the business, for some reason, got bad, and he had to get rid of the trucks. And he went to a horse transportation. So he had four, four pairs of horses, and the people which [INAUDIBLE] those horses. And the same thing-- is strictly for transportation, nothing else. Because there was a railroad station, and there were a lot of businesses which needed the goods transported from one place to the other. And that's what he did. And he did this thing here till he passed away in 1937. And as far as I remember, things were doing pretty good. You know, basic things were doing pretty good. He lost a lot of money when he-- and during the 1932 depression, between '32 and '34. He lost a lot of money there, and that's the reason he has to get rid of the trucks. But otherwise than that, it was basic. I don't know if they had any savings or not. This I don't know. But nobody was starving. Everybody was dressed nicely. And everybody was, thank God, was healthy. And that's far-- far as I know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, prior--

speaker: interviewee
text: --up to that point.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to his going into business, and I-- this may have been before his marriage, he was in-- fought in World War I?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my father was a World War veteran. He was a decorated veteran. He had three medals. And he fought in the Western army. He was an officer in the Western army. He was very, very gravely wounded, really. And he spent a whole full year rehabilitating in a hospital. And I suppose he never recovered from that thing here. Because that contributed to his early departure. He died in August of 1937. But he was a World War veteran, a hero. He could have come to the United States, because his twin brother, who was living in the United States, he-- both of them were serving in the Western army. He got caught by the Russians, sent away to Siberia. He was able to run away from Siberia, retracted back to Poland, from Poland to Vienna, from Vienna to Berlin. And a priest made him out a paper certificate that he'd be able to come to the United States. And, and that's how he came. And he came to the United States in 1920. And as a matter of fact, I had a letter-- he showed me a letter. I don't have the letter, but he showed me a letter which he sent to my father. The kids have that letter now. His sons have that letter now. And that he ask my father to come to the United States. And the request was rejected for one reason. He says, I'm doing fine here. I have four children, and my wife will not leave her parents to go to the United States. So that's the reason he rejected. Of course, if he would have known then what would occur a couple years later, of course he wouldn't have rejected.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meanwhile, was he sick for a while before his death?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father-- was sick only 7 months, gravely sick. And as far as I know, it contributed-- the injury which he had contributed to him. He died from lung disease. In other words-- now, you would have said probably cancer. He was a heavy smoker, too. So now, you would have said he died from cancer, or lung cancer. He-- only seven months. He lasted only seven months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when someone got sick, were they in a hospital? Or were they taken care of at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was in the hospital. As a matter of fact, he-- they asked-- they were looking for help all over the place. He even went to Vienna, because his sister was living in Vienna. And she saw special-- he did see specialists there. And he also saw doctors in Czechoslovakia. And, of course, he had doctors in Bochnia. He spent time in a hospital. And then he was discharged from the hospital and sent home. And that's where he passed away-- at home. But he-- yes he was--he spent time in a hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother who also had passed away, was--

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother--

speaker: interviewer
text: --he in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my brother was in the hospital. And they discovered that he had tuberculosis. And it was dormant, but he never recuperated from it. And it happened through a accident, which he fell from a sled going out-- going back from school. And that developed tuberculosis and something else, and that's why he passed away. And he passed away, actually, in my arms in 1932.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was two years older than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was two years older. He was the oldest. His name was Joseph. He was the oldest. I was then eight years old. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were taking care of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was taking care of them, and I was taking care of two other brothers, and my mother was out shopping. That was a Friday. And it was about 11 o'clock.

speaker: interviewer
text: The way he passed away is very, very unusual. First, he asked me to give him a, a what do you call it? A pot with water. It's a special pot, you know, to wash yourself. But he couldn't-- he couldn't walk anymore. He couldn't walk anymore. So he asked me for that. He asked me for the toothbrush. And he washed himself, and about 10 minutes later, he was gone. And he's still in my eyes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can't forget. Same thing with my father. My father passed away. There were myself there, Uncle David-- which is my mother's brother, the oldest brother-- and my mother, my grandfather, and the doctor in attendance. And it happened-- the doctor was there through whole night, and it happened 6 o'clock in the morning that night. And I was also with him. That-- those memories don't go away, unfortunately. They should, but they don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your mother manage after her husband's death?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother managed strictly whatever she had in saving, I suppose. And then family helped. As you know, in Europe, there was no welfare or something like that. Families always stepped in. And it was a large family on my mother's side, and they were doing pretty good for themselves. And everybody just helped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, her parents were living with you also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, her parents were living with us. Her parents was living with us in that home. And I had also a uncle, what-- there was a building-- apartment to the house, and he had a-- his own apartment, but also in the same building. So there was-- in other words, the families was close. My mother's sister, she had three children. And she had her own apartment, but it was just in the same-- in the-- we-- in other words, the same ground, attached home. And we were living all there. My mother-- of course, my mother's parents were living with us. They had their own apartment. Just, you know, a room. And the kitchen, of course, was shared. And everything was peaceful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, at the time your father died, that was about the same time you finished the grade school? What did you do after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that-- after that, we had a cousin. My grandmother's cousin was living in Tarnow, which is 43 kilometers away from my city south. South of Bochnia 43 kilometers. And he had a big plumbing industry. And it was a big, big industry for the whole area there. And there were-- he employed something like 32 people. And my grandmother, of course, was very close with him. And she called-- and she didn't call, but she strictly went to him and ask for my employment there. That I would like to apprentice. I like plumbing. I like plumbing. I would love to be a plumber for the rest of my life. Anyway, she went to Tarnow, and she approached him, and he immediately accepted me. And, of course, it was a union shop, so I needed all kind of things. But nevertheless, he, he, he accepted me, and I started. About three weeks later, I started to apprentice in plumbing. And I was riding with my train, with a train from Bochnia, to Tarnow, which took about close to one hour away. And it meant-- work started at 8 o'clock there. And it meant to get up very early in the morning. And I did this for about two months, then I simply couldn't continue. I was a small boy. I just couldn't continue. I lost a lot of weight, and I had to-- unfortunately. I was crying, but I had to decide to quit that thing. And besides, right after, you know, when you came back-- by the time I arrived home after work, it was 6 o'clock in the evening. And 8 o'clock, we had to go back to school-- trade school-- and continue our education. But it was trade school. They didn't teach you anything from trade. It was called trade school, but they teach you the continuation of the education. And that has to be done, like, three times a week. So I had to continue with that. And it was just too much. I just had to quit. When I quit that, my uncle which I had, he was living in Silesia. It was approximately 130 kilometers from my hometown. He was a master tailor. And-- he had to be a master tailor. To employ people, you had to be a master tailor. And to take somebody to apprentice, you had to be a master tailor. You couldn't apprentice with anybody else. Otherwise, you would not get your license. Now, before I continue, in Europe, you had to apprentice for three years. You had to apprentice for three years. And after that, you had to also work with a master tailor after that. After the three years, you had to work with a master tailor for about two years-- at least two years. And then you had to produce a full garment. Whichever you choose-- a coat, or a suit, whatever you choose. You had to produce that, and it was scrutinized by other master tailors-- critics. [LAUGHS] If the garment was good, then you passed it. Unfortunately, it was interrupted for me because the war broke out. In any event, I was living with my uncle. His name was Moniek Lehrfeld. That's M-O-N-I-E-K Lehrfeld-- L-E-H-R-F-EL-D. And his wife Helena. And at that time, they had a little baby which was approximately three or four months old. And I was living with him. He had a spacious apartment in upper Silesia. And the town was by the name of Chorzow, which was approximately 3 and 1/2 kilometer from the German border. And I was living and apprenticing with him till the last day of, of August, when the war broke out in September 1st, 1939. I was apprenticing and living with him there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, while you were there, were you aware at all as to what was happening in Germany, and particularly to the Jewish people in Germany? Was there any news coming in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was aware. I was aware for-- by-- for one thing. Yes. I was aware. I was aware also in upper Silesia, because, like, half a year before, there was half of-- in our city, which was Chorzow, half of the population was German, and half of the population was Polish. Now, there were German, German Jews, and Poles, and Polish Jews. To living-- to living there, because it was a border town, you need a special permit. So I had to have a special permit to live there. In any event, about half a year before the war started, I saw myself in our city-- that's the first time which I saw anti-Semitism really spring out. Every Jewish store-- we woke up one morning, and every Jewish store had a sign on the window. Not a sign-- you know, with the-- with the-- black whatever had-- they had. Paint, or whatever they had or they were using that time. I don't know exactly what. And it was strictly-- you know, it was a Jewish store. It was written [POLISH]. That means Jew. And then it was-- they had also different signs like, don't buy-- don't buy by Jew. Buy by your own. They didn't say Pole or German, because they couldn't. So they'd just say, just buy by your own. That was the first thing which I saw real antisemitism in the town. As for what was going on in Germany, I knew also 1938, because one of my uncles married a girl, which-- from across the border. The town of Bytom, which she was a-- she was born German. Her parents were Polish, Polish Jews. But she was born in Germany, and as far as she was concerned, she was German. She didn't even speak any Polish. And in 1938, when the Crystal Night-- just before the Crystal Night, actually, they evac-- they threw all the Polish Jews out. No matter if you're born there or not. If your grandparents were not German, real German Jews, you could not stay in Germany. So they throw all this. They did not accept you as a German citizen. They threw all those people out. They virtually throw them out. They brought them to the borders with the dogs and everything. And-- the town by the name I remember still. It was Bytom. And that's where my aunt went up. And that's how I knew what was happening to the German Jews. As far as in Poland, there was no-- at that time, there was not-- except the scribbling on the-- on the windows, we didn't know anything what was going on. We found that in 1938 that they threw the Jews out. And as a matter of fact, which will come up later, that's how I met the first time my wife when she was a child. And she was carried in on a stretcher, because she developed the pneumonia. She was a very delicate child then, and she developed pneumonia, so she was so weak that they had to bring her on a stretcher. And that's where I found out the troubles in Germany. And, of course, you know, there were writings in the newspaper and everything. But I was not interested in it at that time. I rather looked for the sport pages, and you know, what was the score last week, and so forth, and so on. You know, When you're 15 year-- 15 and a 1/2 years old, you-- or 15 years, at that particular time, you just simply don't have any politics on your mind. So that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, prior to September 1st, was there any advance warning that there would be an attack?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was absolutely not warning at all. As a matter of fact, my uncle was on vacation. If we would have known-- if he would have known that the warning is coming on, I would have not been all by myself in that apartment. So there was absolutely no warning. The only time I saw the first-- my first warning came in the night of the-- of the August. I don't remember it was 30th or 31st. On the 1st of, of January, there was a big commotion on the street. It was 5 o'clock or 4 o'clock in the morning. It was still-- it was not dark. It was light already. And I saw six German march-- you know, marching through the street with their hands up, and soldiers-- border soldiers. And that's the only how I found out that war is coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you found out when you actually saw--

speaker: interviewee
text: When I saw the--

speaker: interviewer
text: --German soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: --the German soldiers. No bombardment, no nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw the soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop on this tape now and continue on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
